messages.content: --- Iteration 1/4 ---
Help me draft an email to our engineering team about pushing the product launch date by two weeks due to a critical bug we found in testing.

Here's a draft email for you:

<draft>
Subject: Product Launch Date Update – Two-Week Postponement

Hi Team,

I wanted to reach out directly to share an important update regarding our upcoming product launch.

During our recent testing phase, we identified a critical bug that affects [brief description of impacted functionality]. After careful evaluation, we've made the decision to push the launch date by two weeks, from [original date] to [new date].

I know this isn't the news any of us wanted to hear, especially given the hard work and long hours everyone has put in to get us this far. However, shipping a product with a known critical issue would put our users at risk and undermine the quality standard we hold ourselves to. Getting this right is more important than getting it out the door on schedule.

Here's what this means for the next two weeks:

- **Bug investigation & resolution:** [Owner name/team] will lead the effort to diagnose and fix the issue, with a target fix completion date of [date].
- **Regression testing:** Once the fix is in place, QA will run a full regression cycle to ensure nothing else has been impacted.
- **Revised milestone check-in:** We'll hold a go/no-go meeting on [date] to confirm we're clear to launch.

If you have questions or concerns, please don't hesitate to bring them to me directly or raise them in our next team sync on [date/time].

Thank you all for your flexibility and continued dedication. We're almost there.

[Your name]
</draft>

A few things you may want to customize:
- **The bug description** — even a vague but honest one-liner helps the team understand the stakes
- **Owner assignment** — being specific about who leads the fix prevents ambiguity
- **Dates** — fill in the original/new launch date and the go/no-go meeting date
- **Tone** — if your team culture is more casual, I can loosen the language a bit

Want me to adjust anything?

The ending is what's really not working for me. It just kind of dissolves — 'thank you for your flexibility, we're almost there' — and after reading a pretty substantive email, that's where I'm left? It feels like the email loses its nerve right when it should be landing. I want to walk away knowing exactly what happens next, not feeling like I just got a pat on the back.

That's a sharp observation — the closing essentially swaps momentum for comfort right when the reader needs clarity most. Here's a revised ending:

<draft>
If you have questions or concerns, please don't hesitate to bring them to me directly or raise them in our next team sync on [date/time].

The next two weeks are defined. We fix the bug by [date], QA signs off by [date], and we make a go/no-go call on [date]. If anything threatens that timeline, I need to know immediately — don't wait for a scheduled check-in.

[Your name]
</draft>

The idea is to close on the schedule itself, not on sentiment. It restates the three critical dates as a tight sequence, and the last sentence makes the escalation expectation explicit rather than leaving it implied. The reader finishes with a clear picture of the road ahead and a concrete instruction.

Want to push it further in any direction — sharper, more direct, different structure?
[Error generating response]

The original launch was October 14th, new date is October 28th. Bug fix deadline is October 18th, QA sign-off by October 24th, go/no-go call October 25th. Marcus is the engineering lead — make him the single point of contact for timeline concerns.

<draft>
Subject: Product Launch Date Update – Two-Week Postponement

Hi Team,

I wanted to reach out directly to share an important update regarding our upcoming product launch.

During our recent testing phase, we identified a critical bug that affects [brief description of impacted functionality]. After careful evaluation, we've made the decision to push the launch date by two weeks, from October 14th to October 28th.

I know this isn't the news any of us wanted to hear, especially given the hard work and long hours everyone has put in to get us this far. However, shipping a product with a known critical issue would put our users at risk and undermine the quality standard we hold ourselves to. Getting this right is more important than getting it out the door on schedule.

Here's what this means for the next two weeks:

- **Bug investigation & resolution:** Marcus will lead the effort to diagnose and fix the issue, with a target fix completion date of October 18th.
- **Regression testing:** Once the fix is in place, QA will run a full regression cycle to ensure nothing else has been impacted, with sign-off due by October 24th.
- **Go/no-go call:** We'll make the final launch decision on October 25th.

The next two weeks are defined. Bug fix by October 18th, QA sign-off by October 24th, go/no-go call on October 25th. If anything threatens that timeline, bring it to Marcus immediately — don't wait for a scheduled check-in.

[Your name]
</draft>

The bullet section and the closing now reinforce each other — the bullets lay out the plan in full, and the closing compresses it into a sequence and points all escalations to Marcus. Let me know if anything else needs adjusting.

The middle section is where I keep getting stuck. Where it explains why we're pushing the date — 'shipping with a known critical issue would undermine the quality standard we hold ourselves to' — that's framed around us, our standards, our reasoning. But the people reading this had things planned around October 14th. Their sprint timelines, their commitments to other teams, maybe customer-facing stuff they'd already communicated. The 'why' should land from where they're sitting, not from where we're sitting.

You're right — that paragraph is essentially asking the team to accept the decision on management's terms rather than acknowledging the real cost they're absorbing. Here's a revised middle section:

<draft>
I know this isn't the news any of us wanted to hear. Some of you have already built sprint plans around October 14th. Others may have made commitments to partner teams or customers based on that date. Those plans don't just evaporate — they have to be renegotiated, and that work falls on you.

We're asking you to absorb that disruption because the alternative is worse: shipping a known critical bug means the problems land on users, and then they land back on this team at the worst possible time. A two-week delay now is a cleaner problem than a broken product later.
</draft>

The shift is that it names the actual burden — renegotiating commitments, redoing plans — before making the case for the decision. That ordering matters. If you acknowledge the cost first, the reasoning that follows reads as honest rather than self-serving. The team is more likely to get behind the call if they feel like it was made with their reality in mind, not in spite of it.

Want me to put the full email together with this version in place?

Okay, the middle is working — that paragraph about sprint plans and renegotiating commitments reads exactly right. But this draft stops before it actually finishes the job. I still don't see where it lands. After explaining why we're doing this, I need to walk away knowing what happens next — specific dates, Marcus as the person to flag anything that threatens the timeline. Right now the email makes its case and then just... stops. The ending is where it needs to close the loop, not trail off.

You're missing the bullet section and the closing from the full email — this draft only has the revised middle paragraph. Here's the complete email with everything in place:

<draft>
Subject: Product Launch Date Update – Two-Week Postponement

Hi Team,

I wanted to reach out directly to share an important update regarding our upcoming product launch.

During our recent testing phase, we identified a critical bug that affects [brief description of impacted functionality]. After careful evaluation, we've made the decision to push the launch date by two weeks, from October 14th to October 28th.

I know this isn't the news any of us wanted to hear. Some of you have already built sprint plans around October 14th. Others may have made commitments to partner teams or customers based on that date. Those plans don't just evaporate — they have to be renegotiated, and that work falls on you.

We're asking you to absorb that disruption because the alternative is worse: shipping a known critical bug means the problems land on users, and then they land back on this team at the worst possible time. A two-week delay now is a cleaner problem than a broken product later.

Here's what the next two weeks look like:

- **Bug investigation & resolution:** Marcus will lead the effort to diagnose and fix the issue, with a target fix completion date of October 18th.
- **Regression testing:** Once the fix is in place, QA will run a full regression cycle to ensure nothing else has been impacted, with sign-off due by October 24th.
- **Go/no-go call:** We'll make the final launch decision on October 25th.

The next two weeks are defined. Bug fix by October 18th, QA sign-off by October 24th, go/no-go call on October 25th. If anything threatens that timeline, bring it to Marcus immediately — don't wait for a scheduled check-in.

[Your name]
</draft>

We're close, but there are two things still nagging at me. The bullet section — the way it's written, each point is describing who owns what role ('QA will run a full regression cycle', 'Marcus will lead the effort to diagnose') in a way that feels like it's mapping out org structure rather than just telling the team what's happening when. It's too much detail about how the work gets done versus what the milestones are. The team doesn't need the email to explain QA's job to them. The dates are what matter — the rest is noise that actually dilutes Marcus being the single person they should flag things to. I want the timeline to feel like a sequence of checkpoints, not a project plan. The second thing is the opening — 'I wanted to reach out directly to share an important update' is basically filler. It's not acknowledging anything, it's just announcing that an announcement is coming. Something that actually names the situation before delivering the news would feel less like a corporate memo and more like a real message.

Both notes land cleanly. The opening buries the news behind a preamble, and the bullets are doing work the team doesn't need — explaining roles instead of just marking the checkpoints. Here's the full email with both fixed:

<draft>
Subject: Product Launch Date Update – Two-Week Postponement

Hi Team,

We found a critical bug in testing. After careful evaluation, we've made the decision to push the launch date by two weeks, from October 14th to October 28th.

I know this isn't the news any of us wanted to hear. Some of you have already built sprint plans around October 14th. Others may have made commitments to partner teams or customers based on that date. Those plans don't just evaporate — they have to be renegotiated, and that work falls on you.

We're asking you to absorb that disruption because the alternative is worse: shipping a known critical bug means the problems land on users, and then they land back on this team at the worst possible time. A two-week delay now is a cleaner problem than a broken product later.

Here's what the next two weeks look like:

- **October 18th:** Bug fix complete
- **October 24th:** QA sign-off
- **October 25th:** Go/no-go call

The timeline is set. If anything threatens it, bring it to Marcus immediately — don't wait for a scheduled check-in.

[Your name]
</draft>

The opening now names the situation in the first sentence before delivering the decision. The bullets are stripped to just dates and milestones — no role descriptions, no process explanation. And with the bullets cleaner, the closing line pointing to Marcus carries more weight because nothing before it has diluted it.

Almost there — the middle and the ending are doing exactly what I need them to do. The one thing that still feels off is the very top. Right now it opens cold: 'We found a critical bug in testing' is the first thing I read, with no setup. It's not that I need a long preamble, but there's something jarring about getting the news before there's even a beat of 'here's what this email is about.' I keep feeling like I'm reading the subject line twice instead of being eased into it. Just one sentence — something that lands me in the situation before the news hits — would make the opening feel less like a door slamming and more like someone actually talking to me.

The fix is small — just one sentence before the news that gives the reader half a step of footing before the floor shifts. A few options for that opening line:

Option 1: "Before we get to launch preparations, there's something important the whole team needs to know."

Option 2: "Testing turned up something this week that changes our launch plans."

Option 3: "I want to make sure everyone hears this from me directly, and not through the grapevine."

Option 2 is probably the strongest — it signals that something has changed without being dramatic about it, and it flows naturally into "We found a critical bug in testing" without feeling like a preamble for its own sake. But Option 3 might be right if the bug has already started circulating informally and you want to get ahead of that.

Which direction feels right, and I'll drop it into the full draft?
Option 2 works. Go ahead and put the full email together with that opener.

<draft>
Subject: Product Launch Date Update – Two-Week Postponement

Hi Team,

Testing turned up something this week that changes our launch plans. We found a critical bug, and after careful evaluation, we've made the decision to push the launch date by two weeks, from October 14th to October 28th.

I know this isn't the news any of us wanted to hear. Some of you have already built sprint plans around October 14th. Others may have made commitments to partner teams or customers based on that date. Those plans don't just evaporate — they have to be renegotiated, and that work falls on you.

We're asking you to absorb that disruption because the alternative is worse: shipping a known critical bug means the problems land on users, and then they land back on this team at the worst possible time. A two-week delay now is a cleaner problem than a broken product later.

Here's what the next two weeks look like:

- **October 18th:** Bug fix complete
- **October 24th:** QA sign-off
- **October 25th:** Go/no-go call

The timeline is set. If anything threatens it, bring it to Marcus immediately — don't wait for a scheduled check-in.

[Your name]
</draft>

The two sentences work together now — the first eases the reader in, the second delivers the news without repeating itself. Everything else stays as it was. Ready to send when you've dropped your name in.

Yeah, this is it. The opener does exactly what I needed — one sentence of framing before the news, so it doesn't feel like I'm just reading the subject line again. The middle paragraph about renegotiating commitments still reads exactly right. And the ending lands cleanly — Marcus, a deadline, done. I'd send this.
--- Iteration 2/4 ---

I need to email a key customer — Priya at Northfield Logistics — letting her know that a feature she requested six months ago is finally shipping next week. She'll need to coordinate with her IT team to enable it, so there's a clear action item on her end.

<draft>Subject: Your Requested Feature Is Shipping Next Week

Hi Priya,

Quick update — the feature you requested six months ago is finally ready and shipping next week. We know you've been waiting on this one, so we wanted to make sure you heard it from us first.

There's one thing you'll need to do on your end: your IT team will need to enable the feature once it's live. It won't activate automatically, so looping them in before next week will help you hit the ground running.

Can you confirm you've connected with your IT team by Friday? Happy to answer any questions in the meantime.

Thanks,
[Your name]</draft>

The bones are there but the ending really bothers me. After the ask, it just trails off with 'Happy to answer any questions in the meantime' — that's the kind of sign-off that makes an email feel like it deflates right when it should land. The CTA is the last real thing Priya should be thinking about, not a soft offer to chat. Can we tighten up the close so it ends on the action, not a pleasantry?

Totally agree — the soft offer undercuts the ask. Here's the close tightened so the confirmation is the last thing she sees:

<draft>Subject: Your Requested Feature Is Shipping Next Week

Hi Priya,

Quick update — the feature you requested six months ago is finally ready and shipping next week. We know you've been waiting on this one, so we wanted to make sure you heard it from us first.

There's one thing you'll need to do on your end: your IT team will need to enable the feature once it's live. It won't activate automatically, so looping them in before next week will keep you from losing time on launch day.

Can you confirm you've connected with your IT team by Friday?</draft>

<draft>Subject: Your Requested Feature Is Shipping Next Week

Hi Priya,

Six months is a long time to wait — the feature is finally ready.

It ships next week, but it won't activate automatically. Your IT team will need to enable it on their end, and if that doesn't happen before launch, you'll lose time on day one.

Can you confirm you've connected with your IT contact by Friday?</draft>

*Draft updated based on rubric changes.*

rubric_revision.change_summary: The new 'Emotional Arc and Sequencing' criterion at priority #1 required restructuring the opening so acknowledgment comes first as its own sentence, followed by the news, then the consequence, then one concrete next step — the current draft blended these together. The updated 'Clear, Single-Owner Action Items' criterion flagged that the CTA implicitly involved a group ('your IT team') without naming a single owner, so the ask was sharpened to name Priya as the accountable party. The 'no_jargon' dimension added to 'Direct, Human Tone' led to removing 'looping them in,' and the updated 'Operational Closings' forward-looking close dimension confirmed the CTA line should stay as the final word with no trailing sentiment.

annotated_changes.original_text: Quick update — the feature you requested six months ago is finally ready and shipping next week. We know you've been waiting on this one, so we wanted to make sure you heard it from us first.
annotated_changes.new_text: Six months is a long time to wait — the feature is finally ready.
annotated_changes.reason: Emotional Arc and Sequencing (new criterion at priority #1, 'acknowledgment_first' dimension): The original blended acknowledgment and news into the same sentence and added a self-centered 'we wanted to make sure you heard it from us first.' The new opener isolates the acknowledgment in one clean sentence per the required sequence, and removes the sender-centered framing.

annotated_changes.original_text: There's one thing you'll need to do on your end: your IT team will need to enable the feature once it's live. It won't activate automatically, so
annotated_changes.new_text: It ships next week, but it won't activate automatically.
annotated_changes.reason: Emotional Arc and Sequencing (new criterion, 'news_before_why' and 'why_before_next_step' dimensions): The news (ships next week) and the consequence (won't activate automatically) needed to be separated from the action item and placed before the CTA. Restructured so news lands first, consequence follows, then the ask.

annotated_changes.original_text: looping them in before next week will keep you from losing time on launch day.
annotated_changes.new_text: Your IT team will need to enable it on their end, and if that doesn't happen before launch, you'll lose time on day one.
annotated_changes.reason: Direct, Human Tone (new 'no_jargon' dimension): 'Looping them in' is corporate jargon. Replaced with plain language. Also sharpens the consequence framing per Reader-Centered Justification (priority #2, 'names_audience_costs'): the burden to the reader is stated directly.

Clear, Single-Owner Action Items (new criterion at priority #4, 'single_owner' dimension): 'Your IT team' is a group ask that diffuses accountability. 'Your IT contact' points toward a single owner without over-specifying org structure.

rubric_revision.original_draft:
Subject: Your Requested Feature Is Shipping Next Week

Hi Priya,

Quick update — the feature you requested six months ago is finally ready and shipping next week. We know you've been waiting on this one, so we wanted to make sure you heard it from us first.

There's one thing you'll need to do on your end: your IT team will need to enable the feature once it's live. It won't activate automatically, so looping them in before next week will keep you from losing time on launch day.

Can you confirm you've connected with your IT team by Friday?

old_rubric.name: Reader-Centered Justification
old_rubric.description: When explaining a decision or making an ask, frame it from where the audience is sitting — including what they've already built, committed to, or been waiting on. Name the specific costs or dependencies they carry before making the case. The 'why' should land from their perspective, not from the sender's. Ordering matters: impact first, rationale second.

dimensions.label: Names specific disruptions or burdens the audience will face as a result of the decision
Acknowledgment of impact comes before the justification, not after
Reasoning is framed in terms of consequences for the reader, not the sender's standards or values
Tone reads as candid acknowledgment rather than minimizing the burden or asking the reader to feel good about it
Where relevant, names pre-existing commitments, workflows, or context the reader has already built around the situation

old_rubric.name: Purposeful Openings
old_rubric.description: Openings should name the situation immediately without filler — no 'announcing that an announcement is coming.' The opening should add framing the subject line doesn't already carry, not restate it. Before delivering hard news, one orienting sentence gives the reader a beat of footing so the message feels like a person talking, not a subject line repeated.

dimensions.label: Opening does not waste a sentence announcing that information is about to follow
The core situation or context is named within the first two sentences
Before hard news, a brief framing sentence eases the reader in without feeling like a preamble
Opening doesn't read as a restatement of the subject line
Opening adds framing or context the subject line doesn't already contain — it advances the reader's understanding rather than restating what they already know

old_rubric.name: Operational Closings
old_rubric.description: The email should land on concrete next steps, deadlines, and accountability — not dissolve into gratitude or motivation. The reader should walk away knowing exactly what happens next. Once a concrete CTA is stated, the message ends — open-ended offers or soft follow-up invitations after the CTA dilute it.

dimensions.label: Final section ends on specific actions, dates, or owners — not sentiment or thanks
Escalation path or point of contact is stated explicitly, not left implied
No motivational filler or gratitude padding appears after the operational close
Key milestones or deadlines are compressed into a tight closing sequence the reader retains
Does not append open-ended offers ('happy to answer questions,' 'feel free to reach out') after a concrete CTA — the action item is the last thing the reader sees

old_rubric.name: Direct, Human Tone
old_rubric.description: The message should read like a person talking, not a corporate memo. Avoid stock phrases and sentiment padding. Use plain, specific language and crisp comparative framing over hedged or abstract corporate vocabulary. The strongest signals of human tone are situation-specific word choices — language that could only have been written about this reader's situation, not recycled from any other email.

dimensions.label: Avoids boilerplate phrases like 'I wanted to reach out,' 'thank you for your flexibility,' or 'please don't hesitate'
Uses concrete, plain language rather than abstract or padded corporate vocabulary
Key trade-offs or decisions are framed in clean, comparative terms rather than hedged explanations
Reads as someone actually talking to colleagues, not issuing a formal notice
At least one specific, situation-anchored phrase signals that the writer is thinking about this reader's context — not filling space with generic reassurance

old_rubric.name: Lean Information Density
old_rubric.description: Timelines and action items should read as a sequence of checkpoints — dates and milestones only. Strip role descriptions, process explanations, and details the audience already knows. Anything that doesn't sharpen clarity is noise that dilutes the signal.

dimensions.label: Action items list dates and outcomes, not descriptions of how the work gets done
Does not explain team roles or responsibilities the audience already understands
Ownership is consolidated to a single person or channel rather than scattered across roles
Every piece of supporting detail either clarifies the next step or is cut

new_rubric.name: Emotional Arc and Sequencing
new_rubric.description: Information must follow a specific order: acknowledge the situation or effort first (one sentence max), then deliver the news, then explain why it matters, then one concrete next step. Violating this sequence feels wrong even if all the pieces are present. This isn't just about completeness — it's about the reader feeling oriented before they're asked to act.

dimensions.label: Opens with a brief acknowledgment of the situation or the reader's effort — one sentence, not a paragraph
The actual news or decision is stated before the explanation — not buried after rationale
Stakes or consequences are explained before the call to action, not after
Closes with exactly one concrete next step — not a list of options or an open invitation
All four elements appear in order: acknowledge → news → why it matters → next step

new_rubric.name: Reader-Centered Justification
new_rubric.description: When explaining a decision or making an ask, frame the 'why' entirely from the reader's perspective — what they've committed to, planned around, or will now have to absorb. The sender's reasoning, standards, or values should not be the frame. If the email centers the sender's logic over the reader's situation, it reads as tone-deaf.

<draft>Subject: Action Required: Remove [Feature Name] from Sales Decks Before Thursday

Hey [Name],

We've had to pull [feature name] from the roadmap — our security team flagged it as a liability we can't ship around.

I know your team has been demoing it to prospects, so I wanted to get ahead of this before the conference. The last thing either of us wants is someone promising something we can't deliver.

Can you make sure the team updates their pitch decks by end of day Wednesday? Happy to jump on a quick call if you need help with messaging around the change.

Thanks,
[Your name]</draft>

The structure feels a little backwards to me. It opens with the bad news before I've even registered that you understand what this disrupts for my team. By the time we get to 'the last thing either of us wants' — that framing is really about us mutually avoiding awkwardness, not about what's actually at stake for the sales team walking into a conference with the wrong deck. That's the part that should land harder. What does this actually cost them if they don't catch it in time? That's what would make me take the Wednesday deadline seriously.

You're right — the current draft leads with the news before the reader is oriented, and the stakes framing is too mutual/abstract. The consequences for the sales team specifically (walking into a conference pitching something that won't ship) need to land before the deadline, not after.

Referencing internal causes (e.g. 'our security team flagged it') is acceptable as brief context, but the consequence framing must return immediately to the reader's exposure, not stay on the sender's side. If the email centers the sender's perspective over the reader's situation, it reads as tone-deaf regardless of intent.

dimensions.label: Names specific consequences, stakes, or disruptions the reader faces — what they'll need to do, absorb, or coordinate
Reasoning and stakes are framed in terms of impact on the reader's world, not the sender's standards, values, or internal decisions

Does not use sender-side reasoning (internal team decisions, organizational standards) as the primary framing vehicle — these may appear briefly as context but consequences must be named from the reader's side
Tone reads as honest about the reader's situation — not minimizing the impact, overselling the upside, or asking the reader to feel good about it
Where relevant, names pre-existing commitments, timelines, or context the reader has already built around the situation

old_rubric.name: Operational Closings
old_rubric.description: The email ends on a concrete next step with a deadline and a clear owner. Once the CTA is stated, the email is over — no trailing sentiment, no 'thanks for your patience,' no open-ended offers to answer questions. The last thing the reader sees should be the action, not a feeling. A brief, action-adjacent offer (e.g. 'happy to jump on a call to work through the messaging') is acceptable immediately before or alongside the CTA if it directly reduces friction on the ask — but open-ended soft offers appended after a complete CTA are not.

dimensions.label: Final section ends on specific actions, dates, or owners — not sentiment or thanks
No motivational filler or gratitude padding appears after the operational close

Does not append open-ended offers ('feel free to reach out,' 'let me know if you have questions') after a concrete CTA — a brief, action-adjacent offer that supports the ask is acceptable if it appears before or alongside the CTA, not after it
Closing line is action-oriented and forward-looking — not backward-looking gratitude or encouragement

old_rubric.name: Lean Information Density
old_rubric.description: Strip anything that doesn't sharpen clarity. Timelines list dates and outcomes, not process explanations. Don't re-explain things the audience already knows. Short paragraphs. Every sentence earns its place.

dimensions.label: Action items list dates and outcomes, not descriptions of how the work gets done

old_rubric.name: Direct, Human Tone
old_rubric.description: The message should read like a person talking, not a corporate memo. Avoid stock phrases, jargon, and sentiment padding. Use plain, specific language. The strongest signal of human tone is language that could only have been written about this reader's situation — not recycled from any other email.

dimensions.label: Avoids boilerplate phrases like 'I wanted to reach out,' 'thank you for your flexibility,' or 'please don't hesitate'
Uses concrete, plain language rather than abstract or padded corporate vocabulary
Free of corporate jargon — no 'synergy,' 'leverage,' 'circle back,' 'alignment,' or similar filler
Reads as someone actually talking to colleagues or contacts, not issuing a formal notice
At least one specific, situation-anchored phrase signals that the writer is thinking about this reader's context — not filling space with generic reassurance

old_rubric.name: Clear, Single-Owner Action Items
old_rubric.description: When action is needed, name one clear owner with a deadline. Don't over-specify to a named individual when the recipient is better positioned to determine internal ownership — 'your IT team' is appropriate when you don't know their org structure. Group asks like 'everyone please review' are non-actions that diffuse accountability. When the recipient IS the owner, the action verb should land on them directly — not be routed through their team.

dimensions.label: Action item names a clear functional owner — a specific person if known, a functional group (e.g. 'your IT team') if not — rather than a diffuse group ask
The action item includes a specific deadline, not a vague timeframe
Avoids 'everyone please...' or other fully diffuse asks where no one feels accountable — a named functional team is acceptable

When the recipient is the owner, the action verb lands on them directly ('Get the decks updated by Wednesday') — not routed through their team ('Have your team update the decks'), which reads as forwarding a message rather than owning a deadline

messages.content:
Here's my feedback on the draft edits:
- Edit "Let's meet Thursday by noon to confirm the revised timeline and settle who owns alerts." → "Confirm the revised timeline and which team owns alerts by Thursday at noon.": The new version is grammatically awkward — 'Confirm the revised timeline and which team owns alerts' reads like a fragment. I also want a single owner on this, not a group ask. Something like 'Can you confirm the revised timeline and alert ownership by Thursday noon?' with a named recipient works better. The original 'Let's meet' was soft, sure, but the fix went too far in the other direction and lost the human voice.

<draft>Subject: Dashboard redesign — scope change and timeline shift

You're both deep into the current dashboard plan, so I want to give you a clear picture of what's changing before it hits your schedules.

The CEO has added real-time alerts as a required feature. That moves the delivery date, and one of your teams will need to own the alerts component end-to-end.

Your sprints are already loaded around the original scope — people are mid-execution, and this lands on top of that. Someone's current work gets displaced, and you'll need to re-sequence before the next sprint kicks off.

I'm not prescribing which team takes alerts; you're better placed to make that call than I am.

Can you both confirm the revised timeline and which team owns alerts by Thursday noon?</draft>

*Draft updated based on accepted rubric suggestion.*
[Error generating response]

The engineering lead is Marcus Webb. He's the right person to own confirming the timeline and alert ownership — he has the most context on capacity. Go ahead and update the CTA to name him specifically.

<draft>Subject: Dashboard redesign — scope change and timeline shift

You're both deep into the current dashboard plan, so I want to give you a clear picture of what's changing before it hits your schedules.

The CEO has added real-time alerts as a required feature. That moves the delivery date, and one of your teams will need to own the alerts component end-to-end.

Your sprints are already loaded around the original scope — people are mid-execution, and this lands on top of that. Someone's current work gets displaced, and you'll need to re-sequence before the next sprint kicks off.

I'm not prescribing which team takes alerts; you're better placed to make that call than I am.

Marcus, can you confirm the revised timeline and which team owns alerts by Thursday noon?</draft>

This one's good — I'd send it. The structure finally feels right: it lands the news, explains what it actually costs them (re-sequencing mid-sprint), doesn't overreach on who should own what, and ends cleanly with Marcus on the hook. No changes needed.

rubric.user_goals_summary: This user writes emails that deliver news, decisions, or action items with a clear emotional arc: orient the reader first, deliver the news, frame the stakes from the reader's perspective, and close on a single concrete action. They value directness without coldness — the email should sound like a person talking, not a corporate memo, but every sentence must earn its place.
rubric.coaching_notes: Non-negotiable: the reader must feel oriented before being asked to act — acknowledge their situation first, news second, stakes third, action last. This user will reject an email that gets the pieces right but the order wrong. Trade-off preference: when directness and human voice conflict, human voice wins — a question-form CTA addressed to a named person is always preferable to a terse imperative that sounds robotic. When unsure about the recipient's internal structure, default to functional group references ('your IT team') rather than inventing specificity the sender doesn't have.
